title: Chapter 38: Sit-In
content: Jordan woke at 6:15 without an alarm.

This was the routine now. Not a compromise. Not a recovery protocol they were adhering to with gritted teeth and white knuckles and the silent promise that they'd stop as soon as they felt better. Just a routine. The way brushing your teeth was a routine. The way breathing was a routine.

They lay in bed for ten minutes. Not meditating — Jordan had complicated feelings about the word *meditating*, mostly that it carried too much baggage from wellness culture and not enough baggage from actual wellness. They were just breathing. In through the nose, out through the mouth, no app, no timer, no voice telling them to visualize a meadow. Just air going in and air going out and the very simple fact that they were alive to notice it.
Then coffee.
Then check the schedule.

Then answer three messages. Not four. Not seven. Three. This was the rule they'd given themselves after the bad month — the month where they'd answered every message, joined every thread, organized eight meetups in fourteen days, and ended up with shaking hands and a resting heart rate of 110 and the distinct sense that they'd built something that was eating them. Three messages. Then stop.

The three messages today were: a thank you to a new group chat member who'd introduced themselves with more enthusiasm than was strictly necessary (Jordan responded with a single 🌿 emoji, which was their version of "welcome, we're glad you're here, please calm down"); a logistics confirmation with the Prospect Park Alliance about the blanket permit (yes, they had a blanket permit now — that's what growth looked like, not more people, but more legitimacy); and a text to their mom saying they'd call tomorrow, which they would, because calling their mom was part of the routine too, and the routine was what kept everything from collapsing.
The stop was what they were most proud of.

Not the breathing. Not the scheduling. The stop. The act of putting the phone down, closing the laptop, and choosing to be done. It had taken six months to learn. Six months of their brain screaming *but what if someone needs you* and *but what if you miss something* and *but what if stopping means you don't care enough.* Six months of sitting with that screaming and not answering it back.
Now the stop felt almost easy. Almost. Like a door that used to be stuck and now opened, but you still had to push.
Saturday. Prospect Park.

Jordan arrived at the Long Meadow at 9:45, fifteen minutes early, which was also part of the routine. They spread two blankets on the grass — not in the most visible spot, not in the most hidden spot, just a spot where people could find them if they were looking and ignore them if they weren't. No sign. No agenda. No pamphlet. They'd tried all of those in the beginning. The sign made it feel like a protest, which it wasn't. The agenda made it feel like a class, which it wasn't. The pamphlet made it feel like a religion, which it really, really wasn't.
It was a sit-in. You sat. That was it.

They'd posted the Google Maps pin on the group chat three days ago, same as always. *Saturday, 10 AM, Long Meadow. Blankets. No sign.* Some people came every week. Some people came once and never came back. Both were fine. Both were the point. You couldn't fail at sitting. You could only stop, and stopping was allowed, and stopping wasn't failure, and Jordan had said this so many times it had become a kind of mantra, except mantras were too organized and this was just a thing they believed.

They settled onto the blanket. Cross-legged. Hands on knees, not because it was the correct position but because it was the position their body found when it stopped trying to be impressive. The grass was slightly damp. The air smelled like cut lawn and distant coffee and the particular green-ness of a New York park in late spring, which was different from the green-ness of forests or fields because it was always competing with exhaust and hot dog carts and somehow holding its own.
Ten AM. People arrived.

The regulars first: Diane from Park Slope, who always brought too much water and shared it anyway. The couple from Crown Heights whose names Jordan kept forgetting but whose faces were familiar and kind. A woman Jordan didn't know, mid-thirties, with the particular stillness of someone who'd been sitting for a long time before she got here — she'd clearly been doing her own version of this.
Then someone new.

He stood at the edge of the blanket like he was calculating whether he had permission to sit on it. Late twenties, maybe twenty-nine. Nice shoes — the kind of nice that suggested he usually wore them to an office, not a park. His posture was the posture of someone used to being good at things and suspicious of things that didn't come with a rubric.
"Is this the, uh—" He checked his phone. "The sit-in thing?"
"Yep," Jordan said.

He sat. Not close, not far. The distance of someone who wanted to be here but didn't want to be seen wanting to be here. He was quiet for four minutes — Jordan counted, not on purpose, just a habit from the early days when they'd timed how long new people lasted before they either relaxed or fled. Four minutes was longer than average.
"I'm Marcus," he said. "Marcus Chen."
"Jordan."

"I'm a data analyst," Marcus said, as if this were relevant. Maybe it was. Maybe it explained the look on his face — the look of someone trying to quantify an experience that refused to be quantified.
"Is there a curriculum?" he asked.
Jordan almost laughed. Not at Marcus. With recognition. The same way you almost laugh when you see a photo of yourself from five years ago and can't believe how much you didn't know.
"No," Jordan said. "You just sit."

Marcus processed this. Jordan could see it happen — the way his brain tried to file 'just sit' into a category and couldn't find one that fit. It wasn't mindfulness. It wasn't therapy. It wasn't activism. It wasn't networking. It was just sitting.
"How do you know if you're doing it right?" Marcus asked.
And there it was. The question. The one everyone asked eventually, in different words, with different intensities. *Am I doing this right? Is there a metric? A goal? A KPI for inner peace?*

Jordan looked at him. Marcus Chen, twenty-nine, data analyst, nice shoes, sitting on a blanket in Prospect Park on a Saturday morning because something in his life had gotten loud enough that he'd Googled 'quiet places Brooklyn' and found their group chat.
"If you're asking," Jordan said, "you're not doing it yet."
Marcus blinked.
"That's okay," Jordan added. "Come back."

They didn't explain further. They'd learned — the hard way, the only way they ever learned anything — that explaining too much defeated the purpose. You couldn't think your way into sitting. You had to sit your way into sitting. The comprehension came later, or it didn't, and either way the sitting was the thing.

Marcus sat for another twenty minutes. Then he left, saying nothing, nodding once. Jordan didn't know if he'd come back. That wasn't Jordan's job. Their job was to be here, on the blanket, breathing, stopping. The rest was up to Marcus.

Marcus's shoes left marks in the grass. Small, deep impressions where he'd planted his feet like he was afraid the ground might leave without him. Jordan noticed this because noticing was part of sitting — not in an anxious way, not in a cataloguing-threats way, but in the gentle, unhurried way you notice things when you're not using your attention as a shield. The impressions would fill with dew later. The grass would spring back. That was how it worked. You pressed yourself into a place, and the place held the shape for a while, and then it didn't, and that was okay, because the pressing was the thing, not the indentation.

The sit-in ran for two hours. Thirty-one people total — a few more than last week, but Jordan didn't track growth because tracking growth was how you turned a practice into a product. Some people talked. Most didn't. A woman near the edge cried for about six minutes, quietly, into her sleeve. The good kind. Jordan could tell by the way her shoulders dropped afterward, like she'd set down something she'd been carrying between her shoulder blades.
After, Jordan walked home through the park. Not quickly. Not slowly. Just walking, which was its own form of sitting if you did it right.

They thought about Marcus. About the way he'd asked *how do you know if you're doing it right* and the answer was: you don't. You never do. There's no moment where a light goes on and someone hands you a certificate that says YOU ARE NOW DOING IT RIGHT. There's just the sitting, and the coming back, and the sitting again, and one day you notice your hands aren't shaking and you can't remember the last time they were.
Jordan remembered being Marcus.

Six months ago. Less, maybe. Organizing eight meetups in two weeks, answering every message, hands shaking so badly they couldn't type, brain running at a frequency that should have been audible. Building something because they thought building was the same as being, and being afraid to stop building because what if stopping meant they disappeared?
Now they sat. They breathed. They went home.
That was the method.
All it ever was.

Evening. Jordan was on their couch, tea going cold because they'd gotten absorbed in a documentary about deep-sea fish, when they thought to take a photo of the park from this morning. Just the blanket, the grass, the Long Meadow stretching out. They sent it to the group chat with no caption.
Chloe's response was immediate: 🍊
An orange. A single orange emoji. Jordan had no idea what it meant and had long ago stopped trying to figure it out. It was just Chloe. It was just enough.
Jordan snorted tea.
_Set the cup down. Let it go cold entirely. Watched the deep-sea fish do their strange, bioluminescent thing in the dark, and didn't check their phone again._
